topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sterile efficiency of a bureaucratic decree. Dr. Albright was departing. My stomach clenched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming just beneath my ribs. I reread the subject line three times, a slow, deliberate motion that felt like an act of pure disdain. He, the gatekeeper to my entire research trajectory, was walking out the door. I leaned back in my worn chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest, and let out a breath that was more of a hiss than a sigh. What a waste of time. All those late-night meetings, the endless, meandering critiques—it all evaporated now, leaving behind only an empty space where direction used to reside. I picked up my pen, the weight of it suddenly feeling insignificant. This entire academic structure, built on the shaky foundation of tenure and mentorship, felt utterly pointless when the architect decided to abandon the blueprint mid-construction.

[story 2]

"So, he's actually leaving?" I managed, the words tasting like ash. My voice was flat, devoid of any inflection that might betray the churning resentment inside me. I watched the reflection of my own face in the darkened monitor screen—pale, drawn, the shadows under my eyes deepening with every passing second. It wasn't sadness that gripped me; it was a cold, sharp sense of vindication. He hadn't been challenging; he had simply been… inefficient. His endless demands for revisions, his patronizing sighs when I presented flawed data—it had all been a performance, a tedious ritual designed to keep me compliant. Now, the performance was over. I stood up abruptly, the scrape of my chair against the linoleum sounding unnaturally loud in the quiet office. I didn't bother to pack my notes; there was nothing left here worth preserving.

[story 3]

I stared at the university crest on the wall, the gold lettering suddenly seeming garish and mocking. The news about Dr. Albright’s departure settled over me like a heavy, suffocating blanket. A slow, simmering heat rose in my chest, tightening my throat. I didn't bother to check my phone; the notifications felt irrelevant. I walked to the window, looking out at the campus quad, where students milled about with a cheerful, oblivious energy. They had no idea what they were missing—the intricate, infuriating dance of intellectual sparring that had defined my last two years. I felt a distinct urge to smash something, anything solid, just to release the pressure building behind my eyes. It was a profound sense of relief, a sharp, almost painful clarity that this entire arrangement, this reliance on an aging, self-important figure, was finally dissolving into dust.
